feedback.text: Greetings! First of all, let me express my sincere appreciation for the initiative of creating a digital services protection act especially in our current times. I would like to provide attention on the subject of addictive design of video games and more importantly how many more protections are required in this field. A particular interesting part of the video games industry is the quite predatory practices of terms of service of current marketed video games. Basically in the current state of affairs, video games can be terminated by their publisher at a whim, while the terms of service do not define an actual end of service. If a video game is defined as a service then it should have a defined end of service term or if a video game is considered a bought arts good then it should not be rendered unusable whenever its publisher wishes it so. For example, when you buy a book, the publisher cannot come to your house and destroy it in an arbitrary number of years after the transaction is complete (or months even) from its release, but that is exactly how video games are currently being sold. On the one hand video game publishers sell video games as art experiences that you can play again and again but on the other hand they still want to attain the definition of a game as a service with an arbitrary life expectancy. That is not in the interest of the customers and it needs to re-assessed. The Stop Destroying Videogames initiative is working towards this and has recently reached over 1.4 million signatures. I believe this initiative aligns really well with the concepts and spirit of the Digital Fairness Act and it can be a great source of information and feedback. Basically, the Stop Destroying Videogames initiative is fighting for fair rights of ownership of video games that can protect customer's from losing access to their bought products or leave the product in a working state after the publisher is done monetizing it (end of life). This type of sentiment resonates in conjunction with the removal of gambling-like features of video games like loot boxes that the Digital Fairness Act is also considering and in total sum, allow for better quality of video game products in general by protecting the customer. Video games are a great industry with a great number of transactions happening daily. By defining a good way to protect the rights of EU customers it will be a great first step in providing a first definition of application ownership and redefine what a software license can entail. As of now software licenses have been used to protect the creators work from being abused by third parties. However, there needs to be a clear distinction between license to use and ownership. I am not saying by any means that the source code of the product should not be protected. However, a bought usage license of a video game should allow the reusability of the product indefinitely (as long as the customer has the hardware to run said video game on their personal devices). This can create a new era of even greater quality of software products for EU citizens not just video games. I hope you give this matter you attention and if my feedback provide any new input, I would say encapsulate the Stop Destroying Videogames initiative to your considerations of items to be viewed on the Digital Services Act. With kind regards and great appreciation, Stylianos Vrontakis
